metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC#2's proof to require three distinct bad-command fixtures (one per defect class: no-assertion, unanchored-grep, unquoted metacharacter), so the verify step actually demonstrates coverage of all three classes the criterion claims.
findings.finding: AC#2's proof specifies 'fixture set with one bad + one unknown command', but the criterion text requires the implementation to 'fire on each of the three defect classes'. A single 'bad' fixture instance does not demonstrably cover all three distinct defect classes (pure file-inspection with no assertion, unanchored-grep patterns, unquoted shell metacharacters), leaving the verify step insufficient to confirm full coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'fixture set with one bad + one unknown command' with 'fixture set with one bad command per defect class (no-assertion, unanchored-grep, unquoted metacharacter) plus one unparseable/unknown command; unit test asserts fire on each bad fixture and abstain on the unknown one.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC2's fixture spec to require at least one representative fixture case per defect class (pure-inspection, unanchored-grep, unquoted-metachar), so all three classes are asserted to fire — not just 'one bad command'.
findings.finding: AC2 specifies a fixture with only 'one bad + one unknown command', but the plan defines THREE distinct defect classes (pure file-inspection with no assertion, unanchored-grep patterns, unquoted shell metacharacters). With a single bad-command fixture, the unit test can go green while two of the three defect classes are never exercised, leaving their detection logic untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'fixture set with one bad + one unknown command; unit test asserts fire vs abstain'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2 proof to: 'fixture set with one example per defect class (pure-inspection, unanchored-grep, unquoted-metachar) + one unknown/unparseable line; parametrized unit test asserts each bad-class fires and the unknown line abstains.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact coverage-dict key(s) (e.g. `linted_commands`, `abstained_commands`) that p6_ac_quality should populate for the verify-command lint sub-check, and add an assertion for those keys in the AC#2 unit test proof.
findings.finding: The plan requires per-command abstains to be 'recorded as coverage', but p6_ac_quality emits a single DetResult with a flat coverage dict (currently `{ran, ac_items}`). The plan does not specify how per-command abstain counts/slots are represented within that single-result structure. An implementer has no spec to follow, and the AC#2 proof test ('unit test asserts fire vs abstain') can be satisfied by a trivial boolean without actually exercising the coverage recording path.
findings.location: ## What — 'a line in an unparseable stack ABSTAINS (coverage), never false-accuses' / AC#2 — 'abstains on unknown/unparseable lines (recorded as coverage)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the coverage dict with `verify_lint: {linted: N, fired: M, abstained: K}` and assert `cov['verify_lint']['abstained'] == 1` for the unknown-command fixture case alongside the fire assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (a) the conditions that cause a verify-command entry to be classified as 'unparseable' (e.g., multi-line continuation, shell compound commands, heredoc), and (b) the coverage dict key(s) under which abstained-line counts are recorded in DetResult.coverage, co-located with the defect-class vocabulary spec.
findings.finding: The plan's abstain/fire decision boundary — 'abstains on unknown/unparseable lines (recorded as coverage)' — references an 'unparseable line' concept whose boundary condition is never defined, and there is no specification of where the per-line abstain count is stored within the existing DetResult.coverage dict schema.
findings.location: What section ('fail-open: a line in an unparseable stack ABSTAINS (coverage)') + AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command and artifact target to AC3 — e.g., 'Proof: the lint function's module docstring or an inline comment block cites shellcheck; grep for the citation passes in CI' — so the criterion is verifiable.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Prior-art note on shellcheck recorded') has no concrete proving command and no specified artifact location. An executor cannot verify completion because it is unclear whether 'recorded' means a code comment, a PR description section, a doc file, or something else, and there is no command to assert it exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'A code comment or docstring at the lint entry point in det_floor.py cites shellcheck and records the adopt/reject rationale. Proof: `grep -n "shellcheck" det_floor.py` returns at least one match.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Disambiguate the ADR 0016 citation in the plan's 'What' constraint by including the full slug (e.g., 'ADR 0016 (project-det-invariants, `docs/adr/0016-project-det-invariants.md`)') to distinguish it from the co-numbered container-leaf-scrutiny ADR.
findings.finding: There are two `ADR 0016` files in the repository (`docs/adr/0016-project-det-invariants.md` and `docs/adr/0016-plan-review-container-leaf-scrutiny.md`). The plan cites 'ADR 0016' as the authority for the DET_CHECKS freeze, which is correct for the project-det-invariants document, but an implementer doing a straight file lookup on '0016' will find two candidates.
findings.location: What section — 'CONSTRAINT: DET_CHECKS in det_floor.py is FROZEN P1-P9 (ADR 0016)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ADR 0016' with 'ADR 0016 (`docs/adr/0016-project-det-invariants.md`)' in the constraint line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the shellcheck-vs-bespoke-regex design decision in the plan before implementation: state which approach is adopted (or explicitly mandate one with rationale), so the executor has a single unambiguous path.
findings.finding: The plan defers the core implementation design choice — adopt shellcheck as a dependency vs. write bespoke regexes — to the executor without resolution. AC3 says 'adopt or justify bespoke,' which is a placeholder for a real architectural decision (dependency management, portability, regex surface area) that an executor cannot resolve without stopping to investigate.
findings.location: What section ('Consider shellcheck as prior art before a bespoke regex set') and AC3 ('adopt or justify bespoke')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a decision line such as: 'Decision: use shellcheck (if available on PATH, else abstain) as the primary engine; fall back to a minimal bespoke regex set for the three named defect classes if shellcheck is absent. Document this in a code comment at the lint entry point.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for the p6_ac_quality extension: specify whether pre-existing violations must be remediated before merge, whether a grace-period warning mode is provided, or how to revert if the new lint rules cause unacceptable false-positive rates in existing CI.
findings.finding: Extending p6_ac_quality with new lint rules is a behavioral change to an existing check that downstream consumers (CI pipelines, test suites) rely on: verify commands that previously passed p6 can now be flagged as failing. The plan has no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequence for this stricter behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that defect-class patterns are stored in a table/registry (e.g., a module-level list of `(name, matcher_fn)` tuples) rather than inline conditionals, so WS5/E6 can extend without modifying p6_ac_quality's internals.
findings.finding: The three defect-class patterns (pure file-inspection, unanchored-grep, unquoted metacharacters) are described as bespoke lint rules but the plan does not specify whether they will be table-driven/configurable or hardcoded inline. Sibling ticket WS5/E6 explicitly extends this same taxonomy, so the ruleset is expected to evolve — hardcoded inline patterns will create churn each time a new class is added.
findings.location: What section — 'Flag: pure file-inspection … unanchored-grep … unquoted shell metacharacters'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level `VERIFY_CMD_LINT_RULES: list[tuple[str, Callable[[str], bool]]]` in det_floor.py and have p6_ac_quality iterate it. New defect classes in WS5/E6 append to the list rather than editing the function body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or expand AC1) to update ADR 0016 (or the relevant design doc) with a note that p6_ac_quality now also enforces verify-command lint, so the ADR remains the accurate source of truth for p6's scope.
findings.finding: The plan requires reconciling with ADR 0016 (don't add P10) and commits to extending p6_ac_quality instead, but includes no commitment to update ADR 0016 or any design doc to record that p6_ac_quality's scope now includes verify-command lint. This leaves the ADR's description of p6 stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` (and any other files that exercise `p6_ac_quality` or `run_det_floor` with plan-text fixtures) in the edit/review scope, and confirm their existing fixtures won't trigger the new verify-command blacklist.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `p6_ac_quality`: it monkeypatches `DET_CHECKS` and runs `run_det_floor` with plan-text fixtures. If existing fixtures contain strings that the new verify-command blacklist flags (e.g., a bare `pytest` command in a 'Proof:' line), those tests could begin failing, but the plan names only `test_det_invariants.py` as the affected test file.
findings.location: What / AC1 — 'Proof: pytest tests/unit/test_det_invariants.py green after update'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` to the plan's file_impact and note that existing P6 fixtures should be audited against the new lint patterns before merging.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the shellcheck-vs-bespoke-regex prior-art decision in the plan itself (not deferred to AC3): either (a) adopt shellcheck with a stated fail-open fallback strategy and output-parsing approach, or (b) explicitly reject it with a rationale (e.g., subprocess dependency is incompatible with the DET floor's no-network, fail-open constraint) and name the bespoke regex set as the sole approach — so AC3 records the outcome, not the question.
findings.finding: The plan names shellcheck as prior art for the three defect classes but defers the adopt-vs-bespoke decision to the executor via AC3 ('adopt or justify bespoke'). This leaves an unverified capability assertion on the table: the plan implicitly assumes bespoke regexes can adequately cover 'pure file-inspection with no assertion', 'unanchored-grep patterns', and 'unquoted shell metacharacters' — all of which shellcheck addresses natively — without resolving whether shellcheck is available in the execution environment, how its output would be parsed into DetResult findings, or why a bespoke approach is preferred.
findings.location: What section — 'Consider shellcheck as prior art before a bespoke regex set'; AC3 — 'Prior-art note on shellcheck recorded (adopt or justify bespoke)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Decision' line in the What section: 'shellcheck is REJECTED as a runtime dependency for p6_ac_quality because the DET floor must fail-open with no subprocess side-effects; bespoke regexes are the chosen approach. AC3 records this rationale inline as a code comment. The three defect-class patterns are: (1) no-assertion heuristic: command matches grep/find/ls/wc/cat/head with no comparison operator or conditional; (2) unanchored-grep: grep invocation with no ^ or $ anchor and no -c/-l/-q flag; (3) unquoted-metacharacter: shell word containing $VAR or `cmd` outside quotes.' This resolves the capability assertion, scopes the implementation, and removes the executor's fork.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the detection vocabulary for each of the three defect classes: enumerate the command tokens / regex patterns for 'pure file-inspection with no assertion', the rule for 'unanchored-grep' (e.g. missing `^`/`$` anchors or no `-x`/`-F` flag), and the shell-metacharacter set considered 'unquoted' — either inline in the plan or in a referenced spec file.
findings.finding: The plan names three defect classes as an enumerated set ('pure file-inspection with no assertion', 'unanchored-grep patterns', 'unquoted shell metacharacters') and AC2 requires the implementation to 'fire on each of the three defect classes', but the plan never defines the detection vocabulary for any of them — no regex patterns, no token grammar, no heuristic boundary. An executor must independently invent all three classifiers, making this an undefined-enum gap in the T8 sense.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Defect-class vocabulary' subsection under What listing: (1) file-inspection commands: {grep,find,ls,wc,cat,head,tail,stat} with no downstream comparison/test operator; (2) unanchored-grep: grep invocation where the pattern argument contains no `^`/`$` and no `-x`/`-F`/`--fixed-strings` flag; (3) unquoted metacharacters: a shell word containing `*`,`?`,`[`,`]`,`$` not enclosed in single or double quotes. Note which of these are shellcheck-delegatable (SC2086, SC2035) vs. bespoke.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
